subject: Chemistry
overall_learning_objectives: Distinguish between pure substances and mixtures based on scientific criteria
Identify homogeneous and heterogeneous mixtures in daily life
Understand the components and properties of a true solution
Learn about suspensions and colloidal solutions
Observe and explain the Tyndall effect
Differentiate between physical and chemical changes
Classify elements into metals, non-metals, and metalloids

lesson_plan.title: What is a Pure Substance?
teaching_strategy: Analysis of food labels (Milk, Salt, Ghee)
Comparison of distilled water vs. tap water
student_activities: Grouping household items into 'pure' and 'mixture' categories
Reading the 'Pure Milk' label and discussing its constituents
assessment: Oral questions: Is air a pure substance?

lesson_plan.title: Types of Mixtures
teaching_strategy: Activity 2.1: Mixing copper sulphate and potassium permanganate in water
Visual aids showing sugar solution vs. sand in water
student_activities: Preparing salt solution and chalk-water mixture
assessment: Classifying a list of mixtures into homo/hetero

lesson_plan.title: Solutions and Concentration
teaching_strategy: Step-by-step calculation of concentration using mass

lesson_plan.title: Suspensions and Colloids
student_activities: Observing the residue on filter paper
assessment: Fill in the blanks on mixture stability

lesson_plan.title: The Tyndall Effect
teaching_strategy: Torch beam experiment with milk/water vs. salt water
Discussion on why we see sunlight 'beams' in dusty rooms
student_activities: Using a laser/torch to test for light scattering in various samples
assessment: Explaining why salt solution doesn't show Tyndall effect

lesson_plan.title: Physical and Chemical Changes
teaching_strategy: Activity: Boiling water vs. burning a candle
student_activities: Categorizing a list of changes (rusting, cutting, freezing)
Writing the definition of a chemical reaction
assessment: Short quiz on properties of compounds

lesson_plan.title: Elements, Compounds, and Revision
student_activities: Participating in a group revision quiz

teaching_aids: Milk, Ink, Salt, Sugar, Chalk powder
evaluation_methods: Accuracy of the Matter classification flowchart
Oral explanation of chemical vs physical change
teacher_tip: Students often struggle with the idea that milk is a mixture. Use the Tyndall effect experiment early on to visually prove that milk has particles large enough to interact with light, unlike a true solution.
